text: This is a video for a combined robotic sigmoidectomy and robotic left hepatectomy by Drs. Chudzinski and Dr. Sukhandi at Florida Hospital Tampa in Tampa, Florida. This is a fifty-six-year-old male who had presented with a sigmoid colon mass approximately forty centimeters from the anal verge. It was biopsy positive for adenocarcinoma. He underwent neoadjuvant chemotherapy with Folfax and Avastin and responded very appropriately. He was presented at multidisciplinary tumor board and was deemed to be an excellent surgical candidate.

The CT scan reveals the tumor in the sigmoid colon. Robotic ports are placed, and laparoscopy identifies the mass and distal tattoo. Peritoneal adhesions are removed using a robotic vessel sealing device. Lighted ureteral stents are enhanced with fluorescent techniques. The inferior mesenteric vessels are isolated and transected with a white load robotic stapler. The mesentery is dissected proximally and distally until negative margins are visible. A green load robotic stapler transects the distal sigmoid colon from the proximal rectum. The specimen is extracorporealized, transected, and an EEA anvil is placed. The descending colon is returned to the peritoneal cavity, and a circular EEA-stapled anastomosis is performed. The pelvis is filled with saline for a negative leak test using a rigid sigmoidoscope.

The robotic system is docked to perform the liver portion of the hepatectomy. Intraoperative ultrasound identifies the liver lesion, and the falciform, left triangular, and coronary ligaments are taken down using monopolar instruments while holding the left lobe of the liver. The gastrohepatic ligament is opened, and the liver transection plane is marked with monopolar instrumentation. The resection plane is initially opened with monopolar instruments, followed by further transection using a vessel sealing device, maintaining hemostasis. A laparoscopic vascular stapling device completes the transection of the liver, dividing the left hepatic vein intrahepatically. Additional suturing and fibrin sealant are applied to ensure hemostasis and prevent biliary leakage.

This is a fifty-six-year-old male who underwent a combined robotic sigmoidectomy and robotic left liver resection. He tolerated the procedure well. The final pathology was T-N-M of invasive moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma. He was discharged on post-operative day three, tolerating a regular diet. His pain was well controlled and he had normal bowel function.
